evidence_quotes.quote: video game preservation, server shutdowns, and digital ownership rights.
intentionally shut down access to video games that players have paid for even single-player titles or those with local offline content.
Modern video games are increasingly dependent on servers, DRM, or online checks to function, even when the core gameplay does not require online connectivity.
remotely deactivate products consumers have legally purchased.

paragraphs.text: I am writing in support of stronger consumer protections in the Digital Fairness Act, specifically regarding video game preservation, server shutdowns, and digital ownership rights. I am part of a growing community supporting the Stop Killing Games initiative, which calls for accountability from companies that intentionally shut down access to video games that players have paid for even single-player titles or those with local offline content. Modern video games are increasingly dependent on servers, DRM, or online checks to function, even when the core gameplay does not require online connectivity. As a result, companies have gained the power to remotely deactivate products consumers have legally purchased. This undermines the very concept of ownership and puts all digital media not just games at risk of being lost due to corporate decisions, profit motives, or cost-cutting. There are numerous recent examples where games were rendered unplayable because the publisher decided to shut down servers, even though no viable technical reason existed to do so. In some cases, these are games that consumers paid full price for, only to lose access to them months or a few short years later. This is not only unfair its a deliberate destruction of digital culture and art. Many affected titles are not "live services" or MMOs, but traditional games that could still be played offline or with minor patches. However, publishers refuse to issue such patches, and in many cases, actively prohibit the community from attempting to restore functionality. They cite vague legal reasons while simultaneously doing nothing to preserve the game themselves. In effect, they are deleting digital property from existence even though players paid for a copy in good faith. This trend is harmful for several reasons: It devalues digital ownership. Consumers lose confidence in buying digital games when theres no guarantee they will be accessible in the future. People arent paying for "licenses to temporary access" they are buying games with the expectation of continued use. It damages digital culture. Video games are part of modern art and history. If companies are allowed to erase them at will, we lose part of our cultural record. No other medium is treated with such disregard. It discourages innovation and preservation. When games are removed from circulation or functionality is revoked, it becomes nearly impossible for archivists, researchers, or even fans to preserve them for future generations. It fosters consumer resentment. Gamers feel betrayed when the products they paid for are killed with no warning or recourse. This creates distrust in the entire digital marketplace and discourages further spending. With these concerns in mind, I urge lawmakers to include the following protections within the Digital Fairness Act: A mandatory obligation for companies to issue offline patches or alternative access methods when shutting down a game's servers especially for single-player or local multiplayer titles. Legal protection for game preservation efforts, allowing third parties (such as museums, preservation groups, or community modders) to legally restore or emulate the servers of games that have been abandoned by their publishers. Required transparency: Publishers should be required to provide clear, advance notice when online services will be terminated and disclose whether the game will remain functional afterward. Clear consumer rights for digital purchases: If a game is being removed or shut down, users should have the right to download a permanent version or receive partial refunds, depending on time owned and accessibility lost. Ban on DRM that prevents local access: If a game is shut down but has no offline fallback, the DRM becomes a tool of digital destruction. This must be addressed. If we dont act now, we risk a future where buying digital games is no different from renting access to
